reasoning: Historically, U.S. credit card debt and revolving plans show steady upward trends structurally due to population growth, inflation, consumer behavior, and economic expansion. Although localized decreases are possible due to cyclical effects, seasonal adjustments handle within-year structural dips (like post-holiday paydowns). Over relatively short windows (e.g., 3-4 months from Nov to Feb), nominal outstanding debt generally grows. Without a stark recession context, the default trajectory for a roughly 3-month window is an increase. I estimate a 75% probability of an increase by the implied horizon given standard macroeconomic trends.
